title-group.article-title: Perioperative Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Burn Patients: Protocol for a Systematic Review

aff.named-content: Department of Anaesthesiology, Surgery and Trauma Centre
Centre of Excellence for Health, Immunity and Infections
Department of Plastic Surgery and Burn Treatment

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Acta Anaesthesiologica Scandinavica published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd on behalf of Acta Anaesthesiologica Scandinavica Foundation.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Burn injuries are associated with substantial morbidity and mortality worldwide, with infections representing a major complication. The desirable and undesirable effects of perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients are unresolved.

sec.title: Methods/Design
sec.p: We will conduct a systematic review with meta‐analysis and trial sequential analysis of randomized clinical trials assessing the desirable and undesirable effects of systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients undergoing surgery, compared with placebo or no prophylaxis/standard of care. The primary outcome will be the number of patients with one or more bloodstream infections. Secondary outcomes include 30‐day and 90‐day mortality, the number of patients with one or more skin and soft tissue infections, ventilator‐associated pneumonias, clostridium difficile infections, antimicrobial‐resistant bacteria, serious adverse events, days alive without organ support (renal replacement therapy, mechanical ventilation, vasopressor), ICU‐ and hospital‐free days. We will search the following databases: CENTRAL, MEDLINE, EMBASE, BIOSIS, Web of Science, and CINAHL. The review will follow the recommendations provided by the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions and the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses guidelines. The certainty of evidence will be assessed using the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation approach.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The outlined systematic review and meta‐analysis will evaluate the desirable and undesirable effects of perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis on patient‐important outcomes and may inform clinical practice and future research in this area.
Trial Registration: ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: CRD420261378296

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Globally, burns are estimated to cause approximately 180,000 deaths each year [1]. Infections constitute a major complication and are among the leading causes of mortality in burn patients primarily due to disruption of the skin barrier and immune dysfunction. These infections may result from microbial invasion of the burn wound, bacterial translocation across the intestinal mucosa, and invasive procedures. Burn wounds are frequently colonized by microorganisms, and surgical procedures may facilitate bacterial translocation into the bloodstream, leading to bacteremia. Consequently, patients with severe burns are at increased risk of developing sepsis and multiple organ dysfunction [2, 3]. Surgical procedures are essential for the life‐saving management of burn patients. However, they may also induce a secondary immune response, potentially worsening the patient's condition prior to wound healing. In this context, increased inflammatory markers are commonly observed [4].

Perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis is the intervention of systemic antibiotics administrated right before or during surgery with the intention of preventing infections. It is applied in a wide variety of surgical procedures, such as thoracic, abdominal, orthopedic, plastic and neurosurgery, where the indications and criteria for use vary depending on the type of surgery [5]. In orthopedic surgery the efficacy has been proven for arthroplasty and traumatology for the prevention of surgical site infections and further bloodstream infections [6].

Despite the clinical burden in burn patients, practices regarding the use of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in this population vary substantially, and the evidence supporting specific treatment regimens is limited [7]. Few studies have investigated the effect of systemic antibiotics in patients undergoing surgical procedures for burns, and the findings are inconsistent. Some studies have demonstrated no statistically significant benefit [8, 9], while others suggest a potential advantage in outcomes such as graft survival [7, 10, 11]. However, concerns regarding colonization with antibiotic‐resistant microorganisms have also been raised. Evidence for this is still lacking [7, 12], but one retrospective study among 5524 burn patients found that bacteria became increasingly resistant as time from admission increased [13].

Given the high risk of infection‐related complications in this population and the inconsistency in evidence and clinical practice, a systematic synthesis of the existing evidence is warranted. We will therefore conduct a systematic review with meta‐analysis and trial sequential analysis (TSA). This approach will synthesize the available evidence on the desirable and undesirable effects of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients and provide a foundation for the design of future trials aimed at informing evidence‐based clinical guidelines in this area.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To evaluate the desirable and undesirable effects of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients compared with placebo or standard care. We hypothesize that the effects of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis on pre‐specified patient‐important outcomes are uncertain due to limited and heterogeneous evidence.
Overall Research Question:
What are the desirable and undesirable effects of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis on patient‐important outcomes compared with standard care or placebo in burn patients?

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: This systematic review will include randomized clinical trials (RCTs) and will incorporate both meta‐analysis and TSA. The methodology described in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions will be applied [14]. The completed systematic review will be reported according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 statement [15, 16]. The certainty of evidence for each outcome will be assessed using the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation GRADE framework [17].

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: The protocol for this systematic review is registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO; www.crd.york.ac.uk/prospero) [18] with the ID: CRD420261378296.

sec.title: Study Types
sec.p: We will include RCTs assessing the effects of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients. Both individually randomized, cluster‐randomized, and quasi‐randomized trials will be eligible. Studies will be included regardless of language, geographical location, source, and publication date. All other study designs will be excluded.

sec.title: Population
sec.p: We will include adult and pediatric burn patients, as defined in the included trials, undergoing surgical procedures regardless of total body surface area (TBSA) affected.

sec.title: Intervention and Comparator
sec.p: Trials in which the intervention consists of perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis will be included, regardless of route of administration, timing, agent, dose, or duration. Perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis will be defined as systemic antibiotic administration given before or during a surgical procedure with the intention of preventing procedure‐related infection, with or without continuation into the immediate postoperative period. The comparator will be placebo or no prophylaxis.

list-item.p: Number of patients with one or more episodes of bloodstream infection (as defined and reported by each trial)
Number of patients with one or more episodes of skin and soft tissue infection (as defined and reported by each trial)
Number of patients with loss of skin graft after surgery (as defined and reported by each trial)
Number of patients with one or more episodes of ventilator‐associated pneumonia
Number of patients with one or more episodes of detection of antimicrobial‐resistant microorganisms (as defined and reported by each trial)
Number of patients with one or more episodes of clostridium difficile infections
Days alive and free from ICU (as defined and reported by each trial; if not reported, ICU length of stay)
Days without organ support including renal replacement therapy, mechanical ventilation, and/or vasopressor
Days alive and free from hospital (as defined and reported by each trial; if not reported, hospital length of stay)
Number of patients with one or more serious adverse events (as defined and reported by each trial)

sec.title: Study Search Strategy
sec.p: A systematic and sensitive electronic database search will be conducted in collaboration with an information specialist. Databases will be searched with no restrictions regarding publication format, date, language, or study design. The full search strategy is available in Appendix 1.

sec.title: Databases
sec.p:
The following databases will be searched:
MEDLINE (1946 to present)
Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL)
EMBASE (1974 to present)
Web of Science.
BIOSIS (via Web of Science)
CINAHL (Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature; EBSCOhost)

Furthermore, a search for ongoing RCTs and unpublished studies will be conducted at the following sites:
Current Controlled Trials (Clinicaltrials.gov)
The World Health Organization International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP) Search Portal (trialsearch.who.int)
The International Standard Randomised Controlled Trial Number registry (www.isrctn.com)
The Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry (anzctr.org.au)
Clinical Trials Information System (CTIS)
European Union Drug Regulating Authorities Clinical Trials Databases (EudraCT).

Studies published in languages other than English will be assessed if accurate translation into English can be ensured using Google Translate. Reference lists of relevant reviews will be screened to identify additional eligible studies. The full search strategy for the included databases will be provided in the manuscript and reported in compliance with PRISMA search methodology [19]. The search will be updated before submission of the review for publication.

sec.title: Study Selection
sec.p: The study selection process will adhere to the methodology outlined in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [14]. Using Covidence, two review authors will independently screen titles and abstracts to identify potentially eligible studies. Studies that do not meet the predefined inclusion criteria will be excluded. Full‐text versions of all potentially eligible studies will be retrieved and assessed independently by the same two reviewers to determine final eligibility for data extraction. In cases of duplicate publications, the report with the longest follow‐up period will be included. Studies will be included regardless of how outcome data are reported. Reasons for exclusion at the full‐text stage will be documented.

Disagreements between reviewers will be resolved through discussion and, if necessary, by consultation with a third review author. The study selection process will be presented using a PRISMA flow diagram. Inter‐reviewer agreement will be quantified using Cohen's kappa statistic.

sec.title: Data Extraction
sec.p: Two reviewers will independently identify, extract, and collect data on a structured, standardized electronic form. Disagreements will be resolved through discussion or, if necessary, by consultation with a third review author. Reviewers will not be blinded to aspects of the reports including study authors, institutions, or journals. To retrieve additional data not reported in the included trials, as well as data from ongoing studies, corresponding authors will be contacted via e‐mail. In cases where multiple publications of the same trial are identified, data extraction will follow the guidance outlined in Chapter 5.5.4 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [14].

We will extract the following data from the included trials: trial characteristics, population characteristics, intervention/control group specifics, pre‐specified outcomes, results data, and methodological details necessary for risk of bias assessment.

sec.title: Risk of Bias
sec.p: Two review authors will independently assess the risk of bias in the included studies using the revised Cochrane risk‐of‐bias tool for randomized trials (RoB 2) [14].

The RoB 2 tool evaluates the following domains:
Bias arising from the randomization process
Bias due to deviations from the intended interventions
Bias due to missing outcome data
Bias in measurement of the outcome
Bias in selection of the reported result

Each domain will be judged as “low risk of bias,” “some concerns,” or “high risk of bias” according to the RoB2‐algorithm based on the responses to the signaling questions. An overall risk‐of‐bias judgment will then be assigned for each study: “Low risk of bias” if all domains are rated as low risk. “Some concerns” if at least one domain raises some concerns, but none are at high risk. “High risk of bias” if at least one domain is rated as high risk or multiple domains raise some concerns.

A summary figure and detailed tables will be presented to justify the risk‐of‐bias judgements for all included studies. Primary conclusions will be based on trials assessed as having an overall low risk of bias. If no such trials are available, conclusions will be based on all eligible studies, with these limitations clearly stated.

sec.title: Measures of Intervention Effect
sec.p: Continuous outcomes will be summarized using means and standard deviations when normally distributed, and medians with interquartile ranges when non‐normally distributed. Categorical outcomes will be summarized as numbers and percentages. For dichotomous outcomes, treatment effects will be expressed as risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). For continuous outcomes, treatment effects will be expressed as mean differences with 95% CIs when outcomes are measured using the same scale, or standardized mean differences with 95% CIs when different scales are used to measure the same outcome.

sec.title: Missing Data
sec.p: We will contact trial authors to obtain any missing outcome data. The primary analysis will include all available data from the intention‐to‐treat populations. Missing data will be assessed according to the Cochrane Handbook. Additionally, we will perform sensitivity analysis using both best‐case and worst‐case scenario assumptions [14, 20].

sec.title: Heterogeneity
sec.p: Statistical heterogeneity will be assessed by visual inspection of forest plots and quantified using measures of inconsistency (I 2) and diversity (D 2) across included trials [21].

Meta‐analyses will primarily be conducted using a random‐effects model; fixed‐effect models will be used in sensitivity analyses [20]. In cases of substantial heterogeneity, potential sources will be explored through sensitivity analyses. Where meta‐analysis is deemed inappropriate, results will be presented descriptively instead. Lastly, the clinical diversity will be measured using the CDIM‐tool, including four domains (setting, population, interventions, and outcome) with 11 items overall [22].

sec.title: Publication Bias
sec.p: Publication bias will be assessed by visually inspecting funnel plots if 10 or more trials are included. Additionally, the Harbord test will be performed for dichotomous outcomes, while the regression asymmetry test will be applied for continuous outcomes [23, 24].

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Statistical analyses will be conducted using Review Manager (RevMan) or R (R Core Team, R Foundation for statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) and the TSA program (Copenhagen Trial Unit, Copenhagen, Denmark). Meta‐analyses will be used to generate pooled estimates for outcome measures that are comparable across at least two studies, provided that the degree of heterogeneity allows for statistical pooling. Forest plots will be generated to display individual trial effect estimates and corresponding study weights for pre‐specified outcomes, as well as the overall pooled effect estimate. When outcome data are unsuitable for meta‐analysis, results will be summarized descriptively. In trials with multiple intervention or control groups, relevant groups will be combined to create a single pairwise comparison between one intervention group and one control group [14].

For cluster‐randomized trials, we will account for the clustered design to avoid unit‐of‐analysis errors. When available, we will extract effect estimates (odds ratios with CIs) and corresponding standard errors from analyses that appropriately account for clustering (multilevel models or generalized estimating equations). If clustering has not been accounted for, we will adjust the results using recommended methods by applying a design effect. Effect estimates will be pooled using the generic inverse‐variance method (section 23.1.3, Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions) [14].

sec.title: Trial Sequential Analysis
sec.p: To control the risks of random errors due to sparse data and repeated significance testing, TSA will be performed [20, 25]. For dichotomous outcomes, trial sequential monitoring boundaries will be constructed based on anticipated relative risk reduction or increase of 15%. For continuous outcomes, a difference of 1 day in ICU‐ or hospital‐free days will be considered the minimal clinically important difference. The type I error rate (alpha) will be set at 5%, and the type II error rate (beta) at 10% (corresponding to 90% power). TSA will be performed for outcomes included in meta‐analyses comprising two or more trials.

sec.title: Subgroup Analyses
sec.p:
The following predefined subgroup analyses of the primary outcome will be performed:
Risk of bias: Trials will be categorized as having “low risk of bias,” “some concerns,” or “high risk of bias”. The “some concerns” and “high risk of bias” categories may be combined if either category includes fewer than 10 trials. We hypothesize a reduced effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in the low risk of bias trials.
TBSA: Trials will be divided into major burns (≥ 20% TBSA) and minor burns (< 20% TBSA). We hypothesize increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in major burn trials.
Mechanical ventilation: Trials including mechanically ventilated patients will be compared with trials including non‐mechanically ventilated patients. Trials that incorporated both mechanically and non‐mechanically ventilated patients will be divided into two groups based on the percentage of patients being mechanically ventilated (higher than 50% or lower than 50% of included population). We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials incorporating mechanically ventilated patients.
Non‐invasive ventilation (NIV): Trials that include patients treated with NIV will be compared with trials that do not. We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials with NIV.
Intervention: Trials including intravenous administration will be compared with trials including oral administration. We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials incorporating intravenous administration.
Population: Trials including adults will be compared with trials including children. In case of trials including both children and adults, they will be assigned to one of two groups depending on whether children or adults constitute the majority of the population. We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials with adults.
Single‐ versus multidrug therapy: Trials using monotherapy will be compared to trials using combination therapy. We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials with multidrug therapy.
Duration of prophylaxis: Trials using systemic antibiotic prophylaxis for ≤ 24 h will be compared with trials using prophylaxis for > 24 h, irrespective of whether prolonged administration was preoperative, postoperative, or both. We hypothesize an increased effect of perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in trials administering prophylaxis for > 24 h.

The primary effect measure will be risk ratios (RR) with 95% CI. Subgroup analyses will be based on pooled RR estimates within each subgroup. Statistical heterogeneity between subgroups will be assessed using a chi‐squared test, with a p‐value below 0.05 considered statistically significant. Given the limited power of this test when few studies are included, results will be interpreted cautiously, with greater emphasis placed on the I 2 statistic. Moreover, the credibility of the subgroup analyses will be assessed using the ICEMAN‐criteria [26].

sec.title: Certainty of Evidence
sec.p: We will assess the certainty of evidence according to the GRADE approach incorporating the five domains: risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and publication bias [17]. A minimum of two authors will independently evaluate the pre‐specified outcomes based on GRADE guidelines. Relevant outcomes will be categorized into one of four levels of certainty regarding the body of evidence: high, moderate, low, or very low. GRADE assessments will be conducted using GRADEpro software, which will also be used to generate the “Summary of Findings” table. We will use the statements described by Santesso et al. to communicate our findings [27].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The proposed systematic review will provide a comprehensive evaluation of the evidence on perioperative systemic antibiotic prophylaxis in burn patients compared with placebo or no prophylaxis and will report on patient‐relevant outcomes. We aim to strengthen the reliability of the systematic review through a rigorous methodological approach conducted in accordance with the PRISMA and GRADE guidelines [14, 16, 17]. The meta‐analysis and subgroup analyses are based on a pre‐specified plan that incorporates patient‐important outcomes. To further support these findings, TSA will be applied to reduce the risk of random errors or premature conclusions and to evaluate the need for additional trials [25].

We aim to include all trials assessing systemic perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis and have therefore adopted a broad definition of the intervention. While this approach increases inclusivity, it may also introduce methodological heterogeneity that could limit the comparability of studies and the interpretability of pooled estimates. Specifically, heterogeneity may arise from differences in patient populations, antibiotic selection, timing and dosage of interventions, as well as outcome definitions and measurements. Therefore, we have implemented our subgroup analyses, the conduction of sensitivity analyses, and measurement of clinical diversity through the CDIM‐tool. The findings of this review may support clinical practice on perioperative antibiotic strategies in burn surgery and identify areas where evidence remains insufficient. Furthermore, the review may highlight the need for greater standardization in future primary studies, particularly regarding antibiotic timing and dosage, outcome definitions, and reporting practices to improve comparability and strengthen the evidence base.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: All authors have contributed to the conception and design of the protocol. Gustav Borghegn Blak Christensen drafted the manuscript. Johan Heiberg is the guarantor. All authors participated in the revision of the manuscript and approved the final protocol.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Disclosure
sec.p: Final version, version 3.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We would like to express our sincere appreciation to Trine Lacoppidan Kæstel, information specialist at Rigshospitalet—Copenhagen University Hospital, for the valuable assistance in phrasing our search strategy.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data sharing not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.
